a0a85a47-cb20-456d-a088-5f8f0949ced0.biography: Working as a Direct Support Professional, Jordan Wagner began his work in the field of intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) more than 15 years ago as a psychology student at The Ohio State University. In this role, he made many friends who had developmental disabilities and assisted them in areas where it was difficult for them to help themselves. He sought to encourage independence for those with whom he worked. After earning his Bachelor of Arts in Psychology, Mr. Wagner now works at OSU Nisonger Center, a University Center for Excellence in Developmental Disabilities (UCEDD), as Project Manager for Nisonger Center Technology Projects. In this role, Jordan incorporates his experience assisting individuals with IDD, his passion for promoting independence, and his enthusiasm for technology. With the collaborative effort of the Technology Projects team, Mr. Wagner has conducted research activities about technology related services to support individuals with IDD and developed educational trainings and experiences, including virtual and in-person experiences with the Smart Home Discovery Place, Nisonger Center’s explorable smart home demonstration environment.

e478af39-09ac-4684-8652-eacbf0a8ac0e.title: Accessibility Engineer & Consultant
e478af39-09ac-4684-8652-eacbf0a8ac0e.biography:
Dylan Isaac is the founder of Enablement Engineering, an AI accessibility consultancy based in Buffalo, NY. Previously Lead AI Engineer at Deque Systems, Dylan invented axe Assistant before turning his focus to building AI systems that extend human capability rather than replace it — a philosophy he calls "enablement engineering." 

His current work centers on Equalify Reflow, a multi-agent pipeline for converting PDFs into accessible, semantic markup. Dylan believes accessibility practitioners have been solving AI alignment problems for decades, and he's here to make that case loudly.

cbc520f6-f132-4f0b-9b1e-3ad6177ae7db.title: Senior Scientist
cbc520f6-f132-4f0b-9b1e-3ad6177ae7db.biography: Daniel Damelin has worked in the field of education for 30 years, as a teacher, curriculum and technology developer, professional development leader, and educational researcher. Currently a Senior Scientist at the non-profit Concord Consortium, he has worked on numerous grant funded projects that resulted in open educational resources to support the learning of science through simulation and data visualization, inquiry-oriented curriculum and pedagogy, and formative assessment.

6b8fd7ae-ef7a-4f46-a15d-91ab6bab1b9e.title: Assistive Technology and Accessible Educational Materials Specialist
6b8fd7ae-ef7a-4f46-a15d-91ab6bab1b9e.biography: Heather Bridgman is an Assistive Technology and Accessible Educational Materials Specialist with the AT&AEM Center at OCALI. She is a rehabilitation engineer and has worked in both the medical and educational environments. Her specialties include computer access, augmentative communication, online learning, and digital accessibility. She has a master’s degree in systems engineering with a concentration in rehabilitation as well as a teaching license in high school mathematics. She is certified as a RESNA Assistive Technology Professional, an IAAP Accessible Document Specialist, and teaches AT courses at Ashland University. Recent projects include development of SIFTS (sifts.ocali.org)  which is an AT feature-matching tool and the online modules known as ATIM (Assistive Technology Internet Modules) https://atinternetmodules.org/. She is also a co-author of the book, Show Me: A Teacher’s Guide to Video Modeling.

8bf49ec6-b777-4d27-b37b-4a0875b5834d.biography:
Mohit Goenka is a dynamic Engineering Leader at Yahoo, where he serves as the Director of Engineering spearheading growth initiatives. With a wealth of experience across Android, iOS, and Web, he has successfully built and led global teams responsible for some of the world’s most complex applications, including Yahoo Mail, Yahoo Messenger, and Grindr. His versatile career spans roles from web developer and product manager to engineering leader, providing him with a holistic perspective on inclusive product development.

A prolific innovator, Mohit is a Yahoo Super Star award recipient and a Master Inventor, holding an extensive portfolio of patents. His commitment to the tech community is reflected in nearly two decades of speaking engagements, including a consistent presence at the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference (2018–2026) and TechEx North America.

Mohit holds a Master of Science in Computer Science from the University of Southern California (USC), where his research focused on Game Theory and Human Behavior. Beyond the office, he is a published poet archived in the USC Libraries, an avid traveler, and a board game strategist.

0b8c70ef-0094-49f0-94d2-f58c1472e24a.title: Accessibility Analyst
0b8c70ef-0094-49f0-94d2-f58c1472e24a.biography: Steve Kerr is an Accessibility Analyst at Google and currently serves as the Vice-Chair of ITI’s Accessibility Committee. With a career spanning technical leadership for accessible procurements in the Canadian federal government to product accessibility at Google, Steve offers a unique, 360-degree perspective on Accessibility Conformance Reports (ACRs) using the Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT®). As a member of the ITI VPAT working group, he helped develop the latest versions of the template. Steve is dedicated to demystifying the VPAT process for both creators and evaluators and has co-developed widely utilized training webinars on the subject. A self-described "conformance nerd," Steve bridges the gap between public sector procurement requirements and private sector product development.

0e10be7c-2faf-48c6-b6d4-8dda42319353.biography:
As a passionate advocate for people-first products, I’ve spent my career at the intersection of technology and accessibility, ensuring that innovation serves everyone. With extensive experience in digital accessibility, software engineering, and a deep commitment to creating inclusive solutions, I’ve worked to harness the power of technology to remove barriers and empower users.

Driven by the belief that accessible products can change lives, I thrive on collaborating with diverse teams to build solutions that reflect the needs of every user. As a technology leader, I focus on leveraging emerging tools and best practices to enhance user experiences while championing inclusivity at every level.

9947e958-e686-45df-a8fa-67998c9ee133.title: PhD(c) in Artificial Intelligence
9947e958-e686-45df-a8fa-67998c9ee133.biography:
Nicolò Donati is a PhD student in Artificial Intelligence at the University of Bologna's Department of Computer Science and Engineering (DISI), focusing on "Generative AI for Innovative Writing and Editing Support Systems." He holds a Master's degree in Artificial Intelligence and a Bachelor's degree in Computer Engineering from the University of Bologna.

Since November 2023, Donati has collaborated with the Language Technologies Lab (LT-Lab), contributing to the automatic generation of educational exercises and content, as well as designing writing assistants aligned with professional editorial standards. In partnership with Zanichelli Editore, a leading Italian educational publisher, he analyzes editorial workflows to identify inefficiencies and applies natural language processing techniques including abstractive summarization and stylistic adaptation to enhance content quality and efficiency. His work leverages large language models to evaluate textual coherence and compliance with editorial guidelines, building toward a robust tool that meets expert-level criteria.​

Donati maintains active research collaborations with industrial partners including Expert.ai, NIER Engineering, BitBang, BiRex, and Bonfiglioli. Beyond his doctoral research, he serves as a teaching tutor in the Department of Pharmacy and Biotechnology at Bologna. His research interests span natural language processing, large language model evaluation, generative AI, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). He has presented work at prestigious venues, including ECAI 2025, where he presented: "Do Large Language Models understand how to be judges?". Donati developed methods to assess whether large language models can function as reliable evaluators for educational writing, particularly for rubric-based assessment. Your work identifies challenges in interpreting hierarchical criteria and cross-genre generalization, while proposing solutions through fine-tuning and diverse benchmarks.

d480001e-9e13-4c71-9281-be76375ae4d9.title: SVP, Global Programs & Regulatory Affairs; European Partnerships
d480001e-9e13-4c71-9281-be76375ae4d9.biography:
Matthew Luken is a Senior Vice President, Global Programs & Regulatory Affairs; European Partnerships, consulting with companies of all sizes, markets, and industries to grow their digital accessibility programs. 

Prior to Deque, Matthew built and ran U.S. Bank’s digital accessibility program, providing accessibility design reviews, compliance testing services, defect remediation consulting, and more. He also served as Head of UXDesign’s Accessibility Center of Practice.

As an expert in digital accessibility, user experience, and service design, Matthew has worked with over 500 brands, covering every vertical and category. He also advises digital designers and accessibility professionals through mentorship programs.

efa78fec-9fe9-47d3-92b8-8d4107a74194.biography:
Kyle Shachmut is a senior leader, community builder and researcher that focuses on digital accessibility in higher education. 

Kyle is the Senior Director of Digital Accessibility at Harvard University, providing strategic direction to digital accessibility efforts across Harvard’s schools. He frequently works at the intersections of information technology, disability inclusion and academic administration. He led creation of Harvard's Digital Accessibility Services team and collaborates with institutional leaders to guide governance of the University's Digital Accessibility Policy. Previously, his focus area included a universal design approach integrating accessibility into at-scale learning experiences through HarvardX—Harvard’s initiative that creates online courses through the Office of the Vice Provost for Advances in Learning.

Beyond Harvard, Kyle is co-chair of the EDUCAUSE IT Accessibility Community Group, the largest affinity group for accessibility professionals in higher education. He has advocated for greater accessibility in community and professional associations for over a decade, advising local state and federal policymakers. In 2021, he received the 'Rising Star Award' among higher ed technology professionals from Educause. 

Outside of higher education, Kyle serves on the Global Leadership Council of the International Association of Accessibility Professionals (IAAP). The GLC provides governance and direction to the IAAP, which his a membership organization that helps advance the profession through certification, continuing education and member support. Kyle frequently consults speaks and writes about matters related to digital accessibility, public policy, technology in education, universal design and more.

c8e0283b-448b-41a3-9e5a-413fabb9cf6e.biography: Taliesin Smith has a master’s degree in Inclusive Design from OCAD University, Toronto, Canada (2016), and a background in linguistics and web accessibility. Her research into non-visual access to PhET’s highly interactive simulations has led to diverse input possibilities through an innovative Parallel DOM architecture (Smith et al, 2018), and a generalizable description design framework (Smith & Moore, 2020) that supports description design for two auditory description features. The Interactive Description feature makes sims accessible to screen reader users, and the Voicing feature delivers descriptions natively through the web browser with simple options to customize how much learners hear.

He was a member of the board of the Austrian Disablity Forum – the Austrian umbrella for organisations for persons with disabilities, representing more the 80 organisations with 1,4 million me60mbers,  in IAAPs Global Leadership Council and in several conference boards (ICCHP, AAATE). He works together with Access Israel and Zero Project, acting as an Ambassador in this world wide-initiative. Klaus has an ICT background, working for nearly 20 years in ICT and standardisation in the context of european (i.e. in the European Disability Forum EDF) and inter/national level (CEN, CENELEC/ISO/ITU). From 2018 to 2020 he was a member of the High Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence at the European Commission. Recently he was selected as Expert of the Accessible EU platform. 204aa8b6-9a3c-4992-a8b4-923adf0eec16.biography:
Judy Thai is the Director of Application Engineering in Digital Innovation & Technology (DigIT) at the University of California Office of the President. With over 25 years of progressive experience at the University of California, she brings deep expertise across the full spectrum of web and application development. She leads multiple teams responsible for enterprise applications that serve the UC's diverse academic and administrative priorities.

Throughout her career, she has championed digital accessibility to ensure technology is inclusive and usable by all individuals, including those with disabilities. Her work reflects a strong commitment to operational excellence, user-centered design, and scalable solutions that advance institutional goals and expand access to opportunity. In her free time, Judy either has her nose in a book, her boots on a hiking trail, or her hands on a video game controller.

ddfc55a8-b408-45a0-8fce-6a43f9ea0b61.biography: Dr. Tina Gonzales Childress is a dynamic audiologist, advocate, and Director of Accessibility for InnoCaption, who bridges audiology, accessibility, and lived experience. A late-deafened adult with bilateral cochlear implants and fluency in ASL, she offers a rare dual perspective across Deaf/Hard of Hearing and Hearing communities. She is also a sought-after international presenter, adjunct lecturer, and mentor supporting individuals of all ages navigating hearing loss. A recognized subject matter expert on assistive and access technologies, Dr. Childress promotes inclusive practices, contributes to Boards and Committees at multiple levels, and creates widely-used resources at TinaChildressAuD.com.

3e3418e7-961f-407e-96df-92befc2e17dc.biography: Kelly Melcher is the Digital Accessibility Coordinator for the State of Minnesota Department of Human Services where she leads the effort to make sure everyone can access, use, and understand the digital content and programs they provide. She is passionate about the idea that we all do better when we all do better. Kelly has worked in digital accessibility for over a decade, and has worked in training and communications for more than 20 years. Kelly is a self-described nerd who loves good conversations about cool ideas.

f632d145-e7ce-4d7d-a9ca-c87b09f69ec1.biography:
Michael Toguchi helps mission-driven institutions modernize their systems so they can focus on their most impactful work. 

As Chief Strategy Officer at Tectonic, he connects strategy, leadership, and business processes with the right technology solutions.  

He speaks to higher education leaders, nonprofit executives, and association decision-makers about using smart systems to streamline workflows, boost efficiency, and improve outcomes without drowning in technical detail.

His approach helps audiences see technology as a strategic tool, not a black box. With 25+ years in education technology and nonprofit operations, Michael has led digital transformation for universities, nonprofits, think tanks, and foundations.  

He oversees Orchestrate a workflow platform that has processed more than 10 million applications and awarded more than $500 million in scholarships, grants and other awards. And Orchestrate AMS, designed specifically for higher education, provides accommodation management, process automation and all the features disability centers need.

My research focuses on exploring communication and expression methods for Deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals who do not use oral speech. I am interested in Deaf and hard-of-hearing communication, welfare informatics, and human–computer interaction (HCI).

fe489cef-aec1-4a2f-876f-7e64ebff57f3.biography: Devon Persing is your neighbor to the north in Seattle, WA, Before joining "big" tech as an accessibility specialist around 2012, she worked in "small" tech as a UI designer, front-end web developer, and usability advocate in libraries, higher education, and local government. She has an MS in Information and is disabled, chronically ill, and neurodivergent. As a result her approach to digital accessibility centers information literacy, organization dynamics, and disability justice. Her current focus as a solo consultant is on helping people do accessibility work that is more holistic, inclusive, cooperative, and sustainable. She wrote a book called The Accessibility Operations Guidebook with that goal in mind.

His research focuses on intelligent robotic systems for electrically powered and sensor-enhanced wheelchairs. This includes driver assistance functions, algorithms for autonomous navigation, and specially adapted user interfaces for people with special needs. Another focus of his work is deep neural networks, particularly the analysis and modeling of medical time-series data.

Daniel Tsivkovski is a Computer Science student at Chapman University with a minor in Information Security and Policy. His technical interests span full-stack web development, app development, assistive technology, and cybersecurity.

He is an undergraduate researcher in the Etezad Lab developing accessible STEM learning software for blind and visually impaired students in grades K-6, and has presented at multiple conferences including SCCUR 2023 and ATIA 2025. The Etezad Lab's research is supported by a $499,134 NSF grant.

ad45ed48-a10e-4ecb-bf50-07fe09bed52d.biography:
Nanci Shepardson is a Reading and Educational Specialist with an M.S.Ed. in Language and Literacy and an Ed.S. in Assistive Technology. She is a certified Dyslexia Practitioner and holds a CPACC credential.

She is a past administrator, seasoned teacher and a credentialed K-12 Reading Specialist. She is a published author, an advocate for students with disabilities and their families, and is also a professional speaker on dyslexia, language-based disabilities, executive functioning, and assistive technology throughout the country. 

Equity and access for all, especially regarding access to research-based reading instruction and assistive technology services, are at the forefront of her work.

She partners with experience and content designers, developers, and testers to ensure accessibility is integrated into every stage of their work. Cassandra has leveraged her seven years of accessibility experience to champion design, usability, and access for all. She holds a BS in Computer Science & Engineering from The Ohio State University and an MS in User Experience Design from Kent State University.

25787b57-6070-4611-8c1c-548463f36cd9.title: Director of Digital Strategy
25787b57-6070-4611-8c1c-548463f36cd9.biography: Simon Miner is the founder of Pedal Point Solutions, an agency dedicated to helping organizations learn, practice, and experience digital accessibility. He is a Certified Web Accessibility Professional with IAAP and has over 25 years of experience in web development and software engineering. Simon has given  presentations and training on web accessibility to corporations, colleges, and community organizations for more than 10 years. He also creates software tools to help people with disabilities better use the Internet such as the Site Unseen and Sound Pointer Chrome extensions, the Song Clock web app, and the award-winning Inclusiville website. He regularly gives talks and presentations at events such as the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference, the Focus on Vision Conference, and the Disabled Stories Collective.

blocks.text: Join us at the 41st CSUN Assistive Technology Conference!
To our dear CSUN ATC Community and beyond,

As the organizers of the annual CSUN Assistive Technology Conference (ATC), we are committed to providing a welcoming, diverse, and inclusive event where every voice is valued. We recognize that recent changes to immigration policy and international travel requirements may pose challenges for some in our community. Yet, our conference is at its strongest when all perspectives—across countries, cultures, and lived experiences—have the opportunity to contribute. We welcome presenters, attendees, companies, and organizations from around the globe, because it is this diversity that makes the CSUN Conference a success.

Accessibility is advancing worldwide, shaped by changing policies and practices. In the past year alone, progress has been marked by the European Accessibility Act, the Accessible Canada Act, India’s Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, and Korea’s Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. The CSUN AT Conference has long been the space where global accessibility leaders gather to share ideas, challenges, and innovations. This year, it is more important than ever for our colleagues from Europe, India, Canada, Asia, and beyond to share insights on how these shifts are influencing product development and shaping more inclusive societies. Together, we can open the door to meaningful, cross-border conversations about compliance, innovation, and inclusion.

Though hosted in the United States, the CSUN AT Conference thrives on international participation. As the largest event of its kind worldwide, it benefits immensely from the exchange of global best practices, groundbreaking research, and emerging technologies that empower people with disabilities across borders. International participants—whether researchers, developers, advocates, or individuals with lived experience—expand the scope of solutions and enrich the dialogue. Their involvement fosters cross-cultural collaboration, promotes universal design principles, and strengthens a global community united in dismantling barriers in education, employment, and daily life.
At its heart, the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference is more than an event—it is a global platform for authentic representation, innovation, and empowerment.
Sincerely,
Julia Santiago, Managing Director CSUN Center on Disabilities
Ted Drake, 2026 CSUN ATC Program Chair
Diane Delaney, 2026 CSUN ATC Program Chair
Klaus Miesenberger, 2026 CSUN ATC Scientific Program Chair
Stay tuned for the latest updates, important deadlines, and exciting announcements by following us on BlueSky Social, Facebook, LinkedIn, and X/Twitter.
For details on sponsorship or exhibiting opportunities, visit our Opportunities page or email us at conference@csun.edu.

We look forward to welcoming the CSUN Conference community back for a 41st year to reconnect, share insights, and continue to build meaningful relationships that drive progress and inspire innovation.
Best regards,
CSUN’s Center on Disabilities Team

a0a85a47-cb20-456d-a088-5f8f0949ced0.biography: Working as a Direct Support Professional, Jordan Wagner began his work in the field of intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) more than 15 years ago as a psychology student at The Ohio State University. In this role, he made many friends who had developmental disabilities and assisted them in areas where it was difficult for them to help themselves. He sought to encourage independence for those with whom he worked. After earning his Bachelor of Arts in Psychology, Mr. Wagner now works at OSU Nisonger Center, a University Center for Excellence in Developmental Disabilities (UCEDD), as Project Manager for Nisonger Center Technology Projects. In this role, Jordan incorporates his experience assisting individuals with IDD, his passion for promoting independence, and his enthusiasm for technology. With the collaborative effort of the Technology Projects team, Mr. Wagner has conducted research activities about technology related services to support individuals with IDD and developed educational trainings and experiences, including virtual and in-person experiences with the Smart Home Discovery Place, Nisonger Center’s explorable smart home demonstration environment.

e478af39-09ac-4684-8652-eacbf0a8ac0e.title: Accessibility Engineer & Consultant
e478af39-09ac-4684-8652-eacbf0a8ac0e.biography:
Dylan Isaac is the founder of Enablement Engineering, an AI accessibility consultancy based in Buffalo, NY. Previously Lead AI Engineer at Deque Systems, Dylan invented axe Assistant before turning his focus to building AI systems that extend human capability rather than replace it — a philosophy he calls "enablement engineering." 

His current work centers on Equalify Reflow, a multi-agent pipeline for converting PDFs into accessible, semantic markup. Dylan believes accessibility practitioners have been solving AI alignment problems for decades, and he's here to make that case loudly.

cbc520f6-f132-4f0b-9b1e-3ad6177ae7db.biography: Daniel Damelin has worked in the field of education for 30 years, as a teacher, curriculum and technology developer, professional development leader, and educational researcher. Currently a Senior Scientist at the non-profit Concord Consortium, he has worked on numerous grant funded projects that resulted in open educational resources to support the learning of science through simulation and data visualization, inquiry-oriented curriculum and pedagogy, and formative assessment.

6b8fd7ae-ef7a-4f46-a15d-91ab6bab1b9e.title: Assistive Technology and Accessible Educational Materials Specialist
6b8fd7ae-ef7a-4f46-a15d-91ab6bab1b9e.biography: Heather Bridgman is an Assistive Technology and Accessible Educational Materials Specialist with the AT&AEM Center at OCALI. She is a rehabilitation engineer and has worked in both the medical and educational environments. Her specialties include computer access, augmentative communication, online learning, and digital accessibility. She has a master’s degree in systems engineering with a concentration in rehabilitation as well as a teaching license in high school mathematics. She is certified as a RESNA Assistive Technology Professional, an IAAP Accessible Document Specialist, and teaches AT courses at Ashland University. Recent projects include development of SIFTS (sifts.ocali.org)  which is an AT feature-matching tool and the online modules known as ATIM (Assistive Technology Internet Modules) https://atinternetmodules.org/. She is also a co-author of the book, Show Me: A Teacher’s Guide to Video Modeling.

8bf49ec6-b777-4d27-b37b-4a0875b5834d.biography:
Mohit Goenka is a dynamic Engineering Leader at Yahoo, where he serves as the Director of Engineering spearheading growth initiatives. With a wealth of experience across Android, iOS, and Web, he has successfully built and led global teams responsible for some of the world’s most complex applications, including Yahoo Mail, Yahoo Messenger, and Grindr. His versatile career spans roles from web developer and product manager to engineering leader, providing him with a holistic perspective on inclusive product development.

A prolific innovator, Mohit is a Yahoo Super Star award recipient and a Master Inventor, holding an extensive portfolio of patents. His commitment to the tech community is reflected in nearly two decades of speaking engagements, including a consistent presence at the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference (2018–2026) and TechEx North America.

Mohit holds a Master of Science in Computer Science from the University of Southern California (USC), where his research focused on Game Theory and Human Behavior. Beyond the office, he is a published poet archived in the USC Libraries, an avid traveler, and a board game strategist.

0b8c70ef-0094-49f0-94d2-f58c1472e24a.biography: Steve Kerr is an Accessibility Analyst at Google and currently serves as the Vice-Chair of ITI’s Accessibility Committee. With a career spanning technical leadership for accessible procurements in the Canadian federal government to product accessibility at Google, Steve offers a unique, 360-degree perspective on Accessibility Conformance Reports (ACRs) using the Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT®). As a member of the ITI VPAT working group, he helped develop the latest versions of the template. Steve is dedicated to demystifying the VPAT process for both creators and evaluators and has co-developed widely utilized training webinars on the subject. A self-described "conformance nerd," Steve bridges the gap between public sector procurement requirements and private sector product development.

Driven by the belief that accessible products can change lives, I thrive on collaborating with diverse teams to build solutions that reflect the needs of every user. 9947e958-e686-45df-a8fa-67998c9ee133.title: PhD(c) in Artificial Intelligence
9947e958-e686-45df-a8fa-67998c9ee133.biography:
Nicolò Donati is a PhD student in Artificial Intelligence at the University of Bologna's Department of Computer Science and Engineering (DISI), focusing on "Generative AI for Innovative Writing and Editing Support Systems." He holds a Master's degree in Artificial Intelligence and a Bachelor's degree in Computer Engineering from the University of Bologna.

Since November 2023, Donati has collaborated with the Language Technologies Lab (LT-Lab), contributing to the automatic generation of educational exercises and content, as well as designing writing assistants aligned with professional editorial standards. In partnership with Zanichelli Editore, a leading Italian educational publisher, he analyzes editorial workflows to identify inefficiencies and applies natural language processing techniques including abstractive summarization and stylistic adaptation to enhance content quality and efficiency. His work leverages large language models to evaluate textual coherence and compliance with editorial guidelines, building toward a robust tool that meets expert-level criteria.​

Donati maintains active research collaborations with industrial partners including Expert.ai, NIER Engineering, BitBang, BiRex, and Bonfiglioli. Beyond his doctoral research, he serves as a teaching tutor in the Department of Pharmacy and Biotechnology at Bologna. His research interests span natural language processing, large language model evaluation, generative AI, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). He has presented work at prestigious venues, including ECAI 2025, where he presented: "Do Large Language Models understand how to be judges?". Donati developed methods to assess whether large language models can function as reliable evaluators for educational writing, particularly for rubric-based assessment. Your work identifies challenges in interpreting hierarchical criteria and cross-genre generalization, while proposing solutions through fine-tuning and diverse benchmarks.

d480001e-9e13-4c71-9281-be76375ae4d9.title: SVP, Global Programs & Regulatory Affairs; European Partnerships
d480001e-9e13-4c71-9281-be76375ae4d9.biography:
Matthew Luken is a Senior Vice President, Global Programs & Regulatory Affairs; European Partnerships, consulting with companies of all sizes, markets, and industries to grow their digital accessibility programs. 

Prior to Deque, Matthew built and ran U.S. Bank’s digital accessibility program, providing accessibility design reviews, compliance testing services, defect remediation consulting, and more. He also served as Head of UXDesign’s Accessibility Center of Practice.

As an expert in digital accessibility, user experience, and service design, Matthew has worked with over 500 brands, covering every vertical and category. He also advises digital designers and accessibility professionals through mentorship programs.

efa78fec-9fe9-47d3-92b8-8d4107a74194.biography:
Kyle Shachmut is a senior leader, community builder and researcher that focuses on digital accessibility in higher education. 

Kyle is the Senior Director of Digital Accessibility at Harvard University, providing strategic direction to digital accessibility efforts across Harvard’s schools. He frequently works at the intersections of information technology, disability inclusion and academic administration. He led creation of Harvard's Digital Accessibility Services team and collaborates with institutional leaders to guide governance of the University's Digital Accessibility Policy. Previously, his focus area included a universal design approach integrating accessibility into at-scale learning experiences through HarvardX—Harvard’s initiative that creates online courses through the Office of the Vice Provost for Advances in Learning.

Beyond Harvard, Kyle is co-chair of the EDUCAUSE IT Accessibility Community Group, the largest affinity group for accessibility professionals in higher education. He has advocated for greater accessibility in community and professional associations for over a decade, advising local state and federal policymakers. In 2021, he received the 'Rising Star Award' among higher ed technology professionals from Educause. 

Outside of higher education, Kyle serves on the Global Leadership Council of the International Association of Accessibility Professionals (IAAP). The GLC provides governance and direction to the IAAP, which his a membership organization that helps advance the profession through certification, continuing education and member support. Kyle frequently consults speaks and writes about matters related to digital accessibility, public policy, technology in education, universal design and more.

c8e0283b-448b-41a3-9e5a-413fabb9cf6e.biography: Taliesin Smith has a master’s degree in Inclusive Design from OCAD University, Toronto, Canada (2016), and a background in linguistics and web accessibility. Her research into non-visual access to PhET’s highly interactive simulations has led to diverse input possibilities through an innovative Parallel DOM architecture (Smith et al, 2018), and a generalizable description design framework (Smith & Moore, 2020) that supports description design for two auditory description features. The Interactive Description feature makes sims accessible to screen reader users, and the Voicing feature delivers descriptions natively through the web browser with simple options to customize how much learners hear.

0dbc9ef2-ba3e-4ae9-8715-25dcbcbc5f66.biography: Klaus Höckner is CEO of the Hilfsgemeinschaft der Blinden und Sehschwachen/Access Austria , based in Vienna. With more than 7000 members and about 70 employees it is the leading privately funded organisation helping visually impaired persons in Austria, existing since 1935. He was a member of the board of the Austrian Disablity Forum – the Austrian umbrella for organisations for persons with disabilities, representing more the 80 organisations with 1,4 million me60mbers,  in IAAPs Global Leadership Council and in several conference boards (ICCHP, AAATE). He works together with Access Israel and Zero Project, acting as an Ambassador in this world wide-initiative. Klaus has an ICT background, working for nearly 20 years in ICT and standardisation in the context of european (i.e. in the European Disability Forum EDF) and inter/national level (CEN, CENELEC/ISO/ITU). From 2018 to 2020 he was a member of the High Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence at the European Commission. Recently he was selected as Expert of the Accessible EU platform. His mission is to make the world accessible for all.

204aa8b6-9a3c-4992-a8b4-923adf0eec16.biography:
Judy Thai is the Director of Application Engineering in Digital Innovation & Technology (DigIT) at the University of California Office of the President. With over 25 years of progressive experience at the University of California, she brings deep expertise across the full spectrum of web and application development. She leads multiple teams responsible for enterprise applications that serve the UC's diverse academic and administrative priorities.

Throughout her career, she has championed digital accessibility to ensure technology is inclusive and usable by all individuals, including those with disabilities. Her work reflects a strong commitment to operational excellence, user-centered design, and scalable solutions that advance institutional goals and expand access to opportunity. In her free time, Judy either has her nose in a book, her boots on a hiking trail, or her hands on a video game controller.

ddfc55a8-b408-45a0-8fce-6a43f9ea0b61.biography: Dr. Tina Gonzales Childress is a dynamic audiologist, advocate, and Director of Accessibility for InnoCaption, who bridges audiology, accessibility, and lived experience. A late-deafened adult with bilateral cochlear implants and fluency in ASL, she offers a rare dual perspective across Deaf/Hard of Hearing and Hearing communities. She is also a sought-after international presenter, adjunct lecturer, and mentor supporting individuals of all ages navigating hearing loss. A recognized subject matter expert on assistive and access technologies, Dr. Childress promotes inclusive practices, contributes to Boards and Committees at multiple levels, and creates widely-used resources at TinaChildressAuD.com.

3e3418e7-961f-407e-96df-92befc2e17dc.biography: Kelly Melcher is the Digital Accessibility Coordinator for the State of Minnesota Department of Human Services where she leads the effort to make sure everyone can access, use, and understand the digital content and programs they provide. She is passionate about the idea that we all do better when we all do better. Kelly has worked in digital accessibility for over a decade, and has worked in training and communications for more than 20 years. Kelly is a self-described nerd who loves good conversations about cool ideas.

6e5e3361-8d17-483a-8757-37a45c7587d4.company: University of California, Office of the President
6e5e3361-8d17-483a-8757-37a45c7587d4.title: Web Accessibility Specialist

f632d145-e7ce-4d7d-a9ca-c87b09f69ec1.title: Chief Strategy Officer
f632d145-e7ce-4d7d-a9ca-c87b09f69ec1.biography:
Michael Toguchi helps mission-driven institutions modernize their systems so they can focus on their most impactful work. 

As Chief Strategy Officer at Tectonic, he connects strategy, leadership, and business processes with the right technology solutions.  

He speaks to higher education leaders, nonprofit executives, and association decision-makers about using smart systems to streamline workflows, boost efficiency, and improve outcomes without drowning in technical detail.

His approach helps audiences see technology as a strategic tool, not a black box. With 25+ years in education technology and nonprofit operations, Michael has led digital transformation for universities, nonprofits, think tanks, and foundations.  

He oversees Orchestrate a workflow platform that has processed more than 10 million applications and awarded more than $500 million in scholarships, grants and other awards. And Orchestrate AMS, designed specifically for higher education, provides accommodation management, process automation and all the features disability centers need.

db765bef-7f44-4391-ac72-b85a0b751c55.biography: I am a first-year Master's student and a Deaf graduate student. My research focuses on exploring communication and expression methods for Deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals who do not use oral speech. I am interested in Deaf and hard-of-hearing communication, welfare informatics, and human–computer interaction (HCI).

fe489cef-aec1-4a2f-876f-7e64ebff57f3.title: Accessibility consultant
fe489cef-aec1-4a2f-876f-7e64ebff57f3.biography: Devon Persing is your neighbor to the north in Seattle, WA, Before joining "big" tech as an accessibility specialist around 2012, she worked in "small" tech as a UI designer, front-end web developer, and usability advocate in libraries, higher education, and local government. She has an MS in Information and is disabled, chronically ill, and neurodivergent. As a result her approach to digital accessibility centers information literacy, organization dynamics, and disability justice. Her current focus as a solo consultant is on helping people do accessibility work that is more holistic, inclusive, cooperative, and sustainable. She wrote a book called The Accessibility Operations Guidebook with that goal in mind.

f4f28c74-84cb-4815-8ddd-ba9f97f583a0.biography: Christian Mandel is a Senior Researcher at DFKI Bremen, Germany. His research focuses on intelligent robotic systems for electrically powered and sensor-enhanced wheelchairs. This includes driver assistance functions, algorithms for autonomous navigation, and specially adapted user interfaces for people with special needs. Another focus of his work is deep neural networks, particularly the analysis and modeling of medical time-series data.

Miller is a Principal Engineer with the Applied Social Media Laboratory at Harvard’s Berkman Klein Center, where he architects technology to serve the public interest. His work focuses on utilizing AI to foster neurodiverse inclusion, healthier interactions online and digital deliberation. A technologist with a civic mission, Brendan applies deep experience from the private and public sectors to these challenges, having served as Chief Software Architect for Fortune 500 clients and as the Director of the Office of Science and Technology for the State of New Mexico. He holds a Math-Computer Science degree from Brown, an MPA from Harvard, and an MBA from MIT.

f3ade242-1efc-4d09-9d72-f18485df0c93.biography: Dean Troup is a graduate from Pennsylvania State University in Rehabilitation Education Services.  He holds two master degrees in leadership and four teaching credentials.  He has been in the k-22 public teaching profession for over 20 years.  Dean has taught as adjunct professor with Adler Graduate College, Brandman University, and Riverside County Office of Education.  Dean studied and obtained the certificate for assistive technology during the pandemic at CSUN in 2020.  He has an Assistive Technology Professional (A.T.P.) Certification and works as an Assistive Technology Specialist Teacher on Special Assignment for Palm Springs Unified School District.  He is the founder of the assistive technology library for Palm Springs Unified.  His email is Deantroup2021@gmail.com

03d62da5-02aa-4215-907d-771534646a6b.biography:
Daniel Tsivkovski is a Computer Science student at Chapman University with a minor in Information Security and Policy. His technical interests span full-stack web development, app development, assistive technology, and cybersecurity.

He is an undergraduate researcher in the Etezad Lab developing accessible STEM learning software for blind and visually impaired students in grades K-6, and has presented at multiple conferences including SCCUR 2023 and ATIA 2025. The Etezad Lab's research is supported by a $499,134 NSF grant. 

Alongside his research, Daniel works as a Software Engineering Intern at Dry Scientific. He also serves as VP of Operations for Chapman's Computer Science Club, where he co-organized PantherHacks 2025 and 2026, the university's student-run hackathons.

Ligia Mora is a strategic visionary with 30 years of software industry expertise and a deep specialization in Document Accessibility over the past seven years. As a Senior Product Development Manager at Crawford Technologies, Ligia bridges the gap between customer needs and technical innovation, collaborating with engineering and sales teams to develop high-quality solutions that streamline workflows and enhance customer communication.

A veteran speaker and technical trainer, Ligia has shared her insights with audiences across the United States, Europe, Latin America, Asia, and Australia. She is dedicated to delivering tailored, accessible solutions that empower organizations to meet the unique needs of their users while maintaining excellence in the document management arena.

ad45ed48-a10e-4ecb-bf50-07fe09bed52d.title: Accessibility and Literacy Specialist
ad45ed48-a10e-4ecb-bf50-07fe09bed52d.biography:
Nanci Shepardson is a Reading and Educational Specialist with an M.S.Ed. in Language and Literacy and an Ed.S. in Assistive Technology. She is a certified Dyslexia Practitioner and holds a CPACC credential.

She is a past administrator, seasoned teacher and a credentialed K-12 Reading Specialist. She is a published author, an advocate for students with disabilities and their families, and is also a professional speaker on dyslexia, language-based disabilities, executive functioning, and assistive technology throughout the country. 

Equity and access for all, especially regarding access to research-based reading instruction and assistive technology services, are at the forefront of her work.

76e4d6c3-775d-4365-96dc-87b2f57e17f9.title: Professor

a9a43f04-ba56-4833-b585-56cec5984dea.biography: Cassandra is a subject-matter expert in accessibility, serving as an internal advisor at JPMorgan Chase. She partners with experience and content designers, developers, and testers to ensure accessibility is integrated into every stage of their work. Cassandra has leveraged her seven years of accessibility experience to champion design, usability, and access for all. She holds a BS in Computer Science & Engineering from The Ohio State University and an MS in User Experience Design from Kent State University.

25787b57-6070-4611-8c1c-548463f36cd9.title: Director of Digital Strategy
25787b57-6070-4611-8c1c-548463f36cd9.biography: Simon Miner is the founder of Pedal Point Solutions, an agency dedicated to helping organizations learn, practice, and experience digital accessibility. He is a Certified Web Accessibility Professional with IAAP and has over 25 years of experience in web development and software engineering. Simon has given  presentations and training on web accessibility to corporations, colleges, and community organizations for more than 10 years. He also creates software tools to help people with disabilities better use the Internet such as the Site Unseen and Sound Pointer Chrome extensions, the Song Clock web app, and the award-winning Inclusiville website. He regularly gives talks and presentations at events such as the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference, the Focus on Vision Conference, and the Disabled Stories Collective.

blocks.text: Join us at the 41st CSUN Assistive Technology Conference!
To our dear CSUN ATC Community and beyond,

As the organizers of the annual CSUN Assistive Technology Conference (ATC), we are committed to providing a welcoming, diverse, and inclusive event where every voice is valued. We recognize that recent changes to immigration policy and international travel requirements may pose challenges for some in our community. Yet, our conference is at its strongest when all perspectives—across countries, cultures, and lived experiences—have the opportunity to contribute. We welcome presenters, attendees, companies, and organizations from around the globe, because it is this diversity that makes the CSUN Conference a success.

Accessibility is advancing worldwide, shaped by changing policies and practices. In the past year alone, progress has been marked by the European Accessibility Act, the Accessible Canada Act, India’s Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, and Korea’s Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. The CSUN AT Conference has long been the space where global accessibility leaders gather to share ideas, challenges, and innovations. This year, it is more important than ever for our colleagues from Europe, India, Canada, Asia, and beyond to share insights on how these shifts are influencing product development and shaping more inclusive societies. Together, we can open the door to meaningful, cross-border conversations about compliance, innovation, and inclusion.

Though hosted in the United States, the CSUN AT Conference thrives on international participation. As the largest event of its kind worldwide, it benefits immensely from the exchange of global best practices, groundbreaking research, and emerging technologies that empower people with disabilities across borders. International participants—whether researchers, developers, advocates, or individuals with lived experience—expand the scope of solutions and enrich the dialogue. Their involvement fosters cross-cultural collaboration, promotes universal design principles, and strengthens a global community united in dismantling barriers in education, employment, and daily life.
At its heart, the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference is more than an event—it is a global platform for authentic representation, innovation, and empowerment.
Sincerely,
Julia Santiago, Managing Director CSUN Center on Disabilities
Ted Drake, 2026 CSUN ATC Program Chair
Diane Delaney, 2026 CSUN ATC Program Chair
Klaus Miesenberger, 2026 CSUN ATC Scientific Program Chair
Stay tuned for the latest updates, important deadlines, and exciting announcements by following us on BlueSky Social, Facebook, LinkedIn, and X/Twitter.
For details on sponsorship or exhibiting opportunities, visit our Opportunities page or email us at conference@csun.edu.

We look forward to welcoming the CSUN Conference community back for a 41st year to reconnect, share insights, and continue to build meaningful relationships that drive progress and inspire innovation.
Best regards,
CSUN’s Center on Disabilities Team
News & Updates
CSUNATC.TV
CSUNATC.TV is now available. Check out our Keynote Address Program and Featured Presentations.
DAISY and Accessible Map
The accessible HTML, EPUB, and DAISY versions of the Conference program and hotel menus are now available for download, courtesy of DAISY and sponsored by ETS.

An Accessible Map is now available for the CSUN AT Conference. Sponsored and provided by XR Navigation, Audiom provides an accessible digital map viewer that is usable visually, auditorily, and through text to identify locations of the conference area and lobby level of the Anaheim Marriott. Visit the XR Navigation website to use the map viewer.
2026 Attendee Registration
2026 Attendee Registration is now closed. There will be no on-site registration. We welcome you to register for the free Exhibit Hall using our Expo Visitor Pass Registration.
2026 Expo Visitor Pass Registration

Expo Visitor Pass Registration for the 2026 CSUN AT Conference Exhibit Hall is now open. Register at no cost to connect with companies, non-profits, and agencies showcasing the latest innovations in assistive technology and accessibility. Visit the Exhibit Hall Directory to review the full list of Exhibitors.
2026 CSUN AT Conference Session Schedule
The CSUNATC26 Session Schedule is now live! Visit the Session Schedule page to review more than 300 educational sessions and explore the available content as you prepare for Attendee Registration.
Latest Journal on Technology & Persons with Disabilities
The Draft of Volume 14 of The Journal on Technology and Persons with Disabilities (PDF) is now available. A final draft of the Journal will be shared post-Conference.

a0a85a47-cb20-456d-a088-5f8f0949ced0.biography: Working as a Direct Support Professional, Jordan Wagner began his work in the field of intellectual and developmental disabilities (IDD) more than 15 years ago as a psychology student at The Ohio State University. In this role, he made many friends who had developmental disabilities and assisted them in areas where it was difficult for them to help themselves. He sought to encourage independence for those with whom he worked. After earning his Bachelor of Arts in Psychology, Mr. Wagner now works at OSU Nisonger Center, a University Center for Excellence in Developmental Disabilities (UCEDD), as Project Manager for Nisonger Center Technology Projects. In this role, Jordan incorporates his experience assisting individuals with IDD, his passion for promoting independence, and his enthusiasm for technology. With the collaborative effort of the Technology Projects team, Mr. Wagner has conducted research activities about technology related services to support individuals with IDD and developed educational trainings and experiences, including virtual and in-person experiences with the Smart Home Discovery Place, Nisonger Center’s explorable smart home demonstration environment.

e478af39-09ac-4684-8652-eacbf0a8ac0e.title: Accessibility Engineer & Consultant
e478af39-09ac-4684-8652-eacbf0a8ac0e.biography:
Dylan Isaac is the founder of Enablement Engineering, an AI accessibility consultancy based in Buffalo, NY. Previously Lead AI Engineer at Deque Systems, Dylan invented axe Assistant before turning his focus to building AI systems that extend human capability rather than replace it — a philosophy he calls "enablement engineering." 

His current work centers on Equalify Reflow, a multi-agent pipeline for converting PDFs into accessible, semantic markup. Dylan believes accessibility practitioners have been solving AI alignment problems for decades, and he's here to make that case loudly.

6b8fd7ae-ef7a-4f46-a15d-91ab6bab1b9e.biography: Heather Bridgman is an Assistive Technology and Accessible Educational Materials Specialist with the AT&AEM Center at OCALI. She is a rehabilitation engineer and has worked in both the medical and educational environments. Her specialties include computer access, augmentative communication, online learning, and digital accessibility. She has a master’s degree in systems engineering with a concentration in rehabilitation as well as a teaching license in high school mathematics. She is certified as a RESNA Assistive Technology Professional, an IAAP Accessible Document Specialist, and teaches AT courses at Ashland University. Recent projects include development of SIFTS (sifts.ocali.org)  which is an AT feature-matching tool and the online modules known as ATIM (Assistive Technology Internet Modules) https://atinternetmodules.org/. She is also a co-author of the book, Show Me: A Teacher’s Guide to Video Modeling.

8bf49ec6-b777-4d27-b37b-4a0875b5834d.biography:
Mohit Goenka is a dynamic Engineering Leader at Yahoo, where he serves as the Director of Engineering spearheading growth initiatives. With a wealth of experience across Android, iOS, and Web, he has successfully built and led global teams responsible for some of the world’s most complex applications, including Yahoo Mail, Yahoo Messenger, and Grindr. His versatile career spans roles from web developer and product manager to engineering leader, providing him with a holistic perspective on inclusive product development.

A prolific innovator, Mohit is a Yahoo Super Star award recipient and a Master Inventor, holding an extensive portfolio of patents. His commitment to the tech community is reflected in nearly two decades of speaking engagements, including a consistent presence at the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference (2018–2026) and TechEx North America.

Mohit holds a Master of Science in Computer Science from the University of Southern California (USC), where his research focused on Game Theory and Human Behavior. Beyond the office, he is a published poet archived in the USC Libraries, an avid traveler, and a board game strategist.

0b8c70ef-0094-49f0-94d2-f58c1472e24a.biography: Steve Kerr is an Accessibility Analyst at Google and currently serves as the Vice-Chair of ITI’s Accessibility Committee. With a career spanning technical leadership for accessible procurements in the Canadian federal government to product accessibility at Google, Steve offers a unique, 360-degree perspective on Accessibility Conformance Reports (ACRs) using the Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT®). As a member of the ITI VPAT working group, he helped develop the latest versions of the template. Steve is dedicated to demystifying the VPAT process for both creators and evaluators and has co-developed widely utilized training webinars on the subject. A self-described "conformance nerd," Steve bridges the gap between public sector procurement requirements and private sector product development.

9947e958-e686-45df-a8fa-67998c9ee133.title: PhD(c) in Artificial Intelligence
9947e958-e686-45df-a8fa-67998c9ee133.biography:
Nicolò Donati is a PhD student in Artificial Intelligence at the University of Bologna's Department of Computer Science and Engineering (DISI), focusing on "Generative AI for Innovative Writing and Editing Support Systems." He holds a Master's degree in Artificial Intelligence and a Bachelor's degree in Computer Engineering from the University of Bologna.

Since November 2023, Donati has collaborated with the Language Technologies Lab (LT-Lab), contributing to the automatic generation of educational exercises and content, as well as designing writing assistants aligned with professional editorial standards. In partnership with Zanichelli Editore, a leading Italian educational publisher, he analyzes editorial workflows to identify inefficiencies and applies natural language processing techniques including abstractive summarization and stylistic adaptation to enhance content quality and efficiency. His work leverages large language models to evaluate textual coherence and compliance with editorial guidelines, building toward a robust tool that meets expert-level criteria.​

Donati maintains active research collaborations with industrial partners including Expert.ai, NIER Engineering, BitBang, BiRex, and Bonfiglioli. Beyond his doctoral research, he serves as a teaching tutor in the Department of Pharmacy and Biotechnology at Bologna. His research interests span natural language processing, large language model evaluation, generative AI, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). He has presented work at prestigious venues, including ECAI 2025, where he presented: "Do Large Language Models understand how to be judges?". Donati developed methods to assess whether large language models can function as reliable evaluators for educational writing, particularly for rubric-based assessment. Your work identifies challenges in interpreting hierarchical criteria and cross-genre generalization, while proposing solutions through fine-tuning and diverse benchmarks.

Matthew Luken is a Senior Vice President, Global Programs & Regulatory Affairs; European Partnerships, consulting with companies of all sizes, markets, and industries to grow their digital accessibility programs. 

Prior to Deque, Matthew built and ran U.S. Bank’s digital accessibility program, providing accessibility design reviews, compliance testing services, defect remediation consulting, and more. He also served as Head of UXDesign’s Accessibility Center of Practice.

As an expert in digital accessibility, user experience, and service design, Matthew has worked with over 500 brands, covering every vertical and category. He also advises digital designers and accessibility professionals through mentorship programs.

efa78fec-9fe9-47d3-92b8-8d4107a74194.biography:
Kyle Shachmut is a senior leader, community builder and researcher that focuses on digital accessibility in higher education. 

Kyle is the Senior Director of Digital Accessibility at Harvard University, providing strategic direction to digital accessibility efforts across Harvard’s schools. He frequently works at the intersections of information technology, disability inclusion and academic administration. He led creation of Harvard's Digital Accessibility Services team and collaborates with institutional leaders to guide governance of the University's Digital Accessibility Policy. Previously, his focus area included a universal design approach integrating accessibility into at-scale learning experiences through HarvardX—Harvard’s initiative that creates online courses through the Office of the Vice Provost for Advances in Learning.

Beyond Harvard, Kyle is co-chair of the EDUCAUSE IT Accessibility Community Group, the largest affinity group for accessibility professionals in higher education. He has advocated for greater accessibility in community and professional associations for over a decade, advising local state and federal policymakers. In 2021, he received the 'Rising Star Award' among higher ed technology professionals from Educause. 

Outside of higher education, Kyle serves on the Global Leadership Council of the International Association of Accessibility Professionals (IAAP). The GLC provides governance and direction to the IAAP, which his a membership organization that helps advance the profession through certification, continuing education and member support. Kyle frequently consults speaks and writes about matters related to digital accessibility, public policy, technology in education, universal design and more.

c8e0283b-448b-41a3-9e5a-413fabb9cf6e.biography: Taliesin Smith has a master’s degree in Inclusive Design from OCAD University, Toronto, Canada (2016), and a background in linguistics and web accessibility. Her research into non-visual access to PhET’s highly interactive simulations has led to diverse input possibilities through an innovative Parallel DOM architecture (Smith et al, 2018), and a generalizable description design framework (Smith & Moore, 2020) that supports description design for two auditory description features. The Interactive Description feature makes sims accessible to screen reader users, and the Voicing feature delivers descriptions natively through the web browser with simple options to customize how much learners hear.

0dbc9ef2-ba3e-4ae9-8715-25dcbcbc5f66.biography: Klaus Höckner is CEO of the Hilfsgemeinschaft der Blinden und Sehschwachen/Access Austria , based in Vienna. With more than 7000 members and about 70 employees it is the leading privately funded organisation helping visually impaired persons in Austria, existing since 1935. He was a member of the board of the Austrian Disablity Forum – the Austrian umbrella for organisations for persons with disabilities, representing more the 80 organisations with 1,4 million me60mbers,  in IAAPs Global Leadership Council and in several conference boards (ICCHP, AAATE). He works together with Access Israel and Zero Project, acting as an Ambassador in this world wide-initiative. Klaus has an ICT background, working for nearly 20 years in ICT and standardisation in the context of european (i.e. in the European Disability Forum EDF) and inter/national level (CEN, CENELEC/ISO/ITU). From 2018 to 2020 he was a member of the High Level Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence at the European Commission. Recently he was selected as Expert of the Accessible EU platform. His mission is to make the world accessible for all.

Judy Thai is the Director of Application Engineering in Digital Innovation & Technology (DigIT) at the University of California Office of the President. With over 25 years of progressive experience at the University of California, she brings deep expertise across the full spectrum of web and application development. She leads multiple teams responsible for enterprise applications that serve the UC's diverse academic and administrative priorities.

Throughout her career, she has championed digital accessibility to ensure technology is inclusive and usable by all individuals, including those with disabilities. Her work reflects a strong commitment to operational excellence, user-centered design, and scalable solutions that advance institutional goals and expand access to opportunity. In her free time, Judy either has her nose in a book, her boots on a hiking trail, or her hands on a video game controller.

ddfc55a8-b408-45a0-8fce-6a43f9ea0b61.biography: Dr. Tina Gonzales Childress is a dynamic audiologist, advocate, and Director of Accessibility for InnoCaption, who bridges audiology, accessibility, and lived experience. A late-deafened adult with bilateral cochlear implants and fluency in ASL, she offers a rare dual perspective across Deaf/Hard of Hearing and Hearing communities. She is also a sought-after international presenter, adjunct lecturer, and mentor supporting individuals of all ages navigating hearing loss. A recognized subject matter expert on assistive and access technologies, Dr. Childress promotes inclusive practices, contributes to Boards and Committees at multiple levels, and creates widely-used resources at TinaChildressAuD.com.

She is passionate about the idea that we all do better when we all do better. Kelly has worked in digital accessibility for over a decade, and has worked in training and communications for more than 20 years. Kelly is a self-described nerd who loves good conversations about cool ideas.

f632d145-e7ce-4d7d-a9ca-c87b09f69ec1.biography:
Michael Toguchi helps mission-driven institutions modernize their systems so they can focus on their most impactful work. 

As Chief Strategy Officer at Tectonic, he connects strategy, leadership, and business processes with the right technology solutions.  

He speaks to higher education leaders, nonprofit executives, and association decision-makers about using smart systems to streamline workflows, boost efficiency, and improve outcomes without drowning in technical detail.

His approach helps audiences see technology as a strategic tool, not a black box. With 25+ years in education technology and nonprofit operations, Michael has led digital transformation for universities, nonprofits, think tanks, and foundations.  

He oversees Orchestrate a workflow platform that has processed more than 10 million applications and awarded more than $500 million in scholarships, grants and other awards. And Orchestrate AMS, designed specifically for higher education, provides accommodation management, process automation and all the features disability centers need.

db765bef-7f44-4391-ac72-b85a0b751c55.biography: I am a first-year Master's student and a Deaf graduate student. My research focuses on exploring communication and expression methods for Deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals who do not use oral speech. I am interested in Deaf and hard-of-hearing communication, welfare informatics, and human–computer interaction (HCI).

fe489cef-aec1-4a2f-876f-7e64ebff57f3.biography: Devon Persing is your neighbor to the north in Seattle, WA, Before joining "big" tech as an accessibility specialist around 2012, she worked in "small" tech as a UI designer, front-end web developer, and usability advocate in libraries, higher education, and local government. She has an MS in Information and is disabled, chronically ill, and neurodivergent. As a result her approach to digital accessibility centers information literacy, organization dynamics, and disability justice. Her current focus as a solo consultant is on helping people do accessibility work that is more holistic, inclusive, cooperative, and sustainable. She wrote a book called The Accessibility Operations Guidebook with that goal in mind.

His research focuses on intelligent robotic systems for electrically powered and sensor-enhanced wheelchairs. This includes driver assistance functions, algorithms for autonomous navigation, and specially adapted user interfaces for people with special needs. Another focus of his work is deep neural networks, particularly the analysis and modeling of medical time-series data.

He is an undergraduate researcher in the Etezad Lab developing accessible STEM learning software for blind and visually impaired students in grades K-6, and has presented at multiple conferences including SCCUR 2023 and ATIA 2025. The Etezad Lab's research is supported by a $499,134 NSF grant.

ad45ed48-a10e-4ecb-bf50-07fe09bed52d.biography:
Nanci Shepardson is a Reading and Educational Specialist with an M.S.Ed. in Language and Literacy and an Ed.S. in Assistive Technology. She is a certified Dyslexia Practitioner and holds a CPACC credential.

She is a past administrator, seasoned teacher and a credentialed K-12 Reading Specialist. She is a published author, an advocate for students with disabilities and their families, and is also a professional speaker on dyslexia, language-based disabilities, executive functioning, and assistive technology throughout the country. 

Equity and access for all, especially regarding access to research-based reading instruction and assistive technology services, are at the forefront of her work.

She partners with experience and content designers, developers, and testers to ensure accessibility is integrated into every stage of their work. Cassandra has leveraged her seven years of accessibility experience to champion design, usability, and access for all. She holds a BS in Computer Science & Engineering from The Ohio State University and an MS in User Experience Design from Kent State University.

25787b57-6070-4611-8c1c-548463f36cd9.biography: Simon Miner is the founder of Pedal Point Solutions, an agency dedicated to helping organizations learn, practice, and experience digital accessibility. He is a Certified Web Accessibility Professional with IAAP and has over 25 years of experience in web development and software engineering. Simon has given  presentations and training on web accessibility to corporations, colleges, and community organizations for more than 10 years. He also creates software tools to help people with disabilities better use the Internet such as the Site Unseen and Sound Pointer Chrome extensions, the Song Clock web app, and the award-winning Inclusiville website. He regularly gives talks and presentations at events such as the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference, the Focus on Vision Conference, and the Disabled Stories Collective.

blocks.text: Join us at the 41st CSUN Assistive Technology Conference!
To our dear CSUN ATC Community and beyond,

As the organizers of the annual CSUN Assistive Technology Conference (ATC), we are committed to providing a welcoming, diverse, and inclusive event where every voice is valued. We recognize that recent changes to immigration policy and international travel requirements may pose challenges for some in our community. Yet, our conference is at its strongest when all perspectives—across countries, cultures, and lived experiences—have the opportunity to contribute. We welcome presenters, attendees, companies, and organizations from around the globe, because it is this diversity that makes the CSUN Conference a success.

Accessibility is advancing worldwide, shaped by changing policies and practices. In the past year alone, progress has been marked by the European Accessibility Act, the Accessible Canada Act, India’s Rights of Persons with Disabilities Act, and Korea’s Web Content Accessibility Guidelines. The CSUN AT Conference has long been the space where global accessibility leaders gather to share ideas, challenges, and innovations. This year, it is more important than ever for our colleagues from Europe, India, Canada, Asia, and beyond to share insights on how these shifts are influencing product development and shaping more inclusive societies. Together, we can open the door to meaningful, cross-border conversations about compliance, innovation, and inclusion.

Though hosted in the United States, the CSUN AT Conference thrives on international participation. As the largest event of its kind worldwide, it benefits immensely from the exchange of global best practices, groundbreaking research, and emerging technologies that empower people with disabilities across borders. International participants—whether researchers, developers, advocates, or individuals with lived experience—expand the scope of solutions and enrich the dialogue. Their involvement fosters cross-cultural collaboration, promotes universal design principles, and strengthens a global community united in dismantling barriers in education, employment, and daily life.
At its heart, the CSUN Assistive Technology Conference is more than an event—it is a global platform for authentic representation, innovation, and empowerment.
Sincerely,
Julia Santiago, Managing Director CSUN Center on Disabilities
Ted Drake, 2026 CSUN ATC Program Chair
Diane Delaney, 2026 CSUN ATC Program Chair
Klaus Miesenberger, 2026 CSUN ATC Scientific Program Chair
Stay tuned for the latest updates, important deadlines, and exciting announcements by following us on BlueSky Social, Facebook, LinkedIn, and X/Twitter.
For details on sponsorship or exhibiting opportunities, visit our Opportunities page or email us at conference@csun.edu.

We look forward to welcoming the CSUN Conference community back for a 41st year to reconnect, share insights, and continue to build meaningful relationships that drive progress and inspire innovation.
Best regards,
CSUN’s Center on Disabilities Team
